''Norito'' were (and still are) traditionally written in a variety of ''[[man'yōgana]]'' where [[Japanese particles|particles]] and suffixes are written in a smaller script than the main body of the text.<ref>{{cite book|editor1-last=Sinor|editor1-first=Denis|title=American Oriental Society, Middle West Branch, Semi-Centennial Volume: A Collection of Original Essays|date=1969|publisher=Indiana University Press|pages=242–243|isbn=978-0-253-39503-0 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=vGEDAAAAMAAJ}}</ref> This style of writing, used in imperial edicts (宣命 ''senmyō'') preserved in the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' and other texts dating from the 8th century ([[Nara period]]), is known as ''senmyōgaki''.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Seeley|first1=Christopher|title=A History of Writing in Japan|date=1991|publisher=Brill|isbn=978-9004090811|pages=54–55|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KCZ2ya6cg88C&pg=PA54}}</ref>

{{Short description|Liturgical texts or ritual incantations in Shinto}} {{lacking overview|date=April 2014}} {{Shinto}} {{Nihongo|'''Norito'''|祝詞}} are [[Liturgy|liturgical]] texts or ritual incantations in [[Shinto]], usually addressed to a given ''[[kami]]''.<ref>{{cite book|last=Philippi|first=Donald L.|title=Norito: A Translation of the Ancient Japanese Ritual Prayers|year=1990|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=0691014892|page=vii}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|title=Norito|url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/norito|website=Encyclopedia Britannica}}</ref><ref name="EncyclopediaShinto">{{cite web|last1=Motosawa|first1=Masafumi|title=Norito|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1246|website=Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Kokugakuin University}}</ref>
== History == The first written documentation of ''norito'' dates to 712 CE in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and 720 CE in the ''[[Nihongi]]''.<ref name="EncyclopediaShinto" />
The [[Engishiki]], a compilation of laws and minute regulation presented by the court compiled in 927 CE, preserves twenty-seven representative forms of ''norito''.<ref>Philippi (1990). p. 1.</ref><ref>{{cite book|last1=Kitagawa|first1=Joseph Mitsuo|title=On Understanding Japanese Religion|date=1987|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=978-0691102290|page=[https://archive.org/details/onunderstandingj0000kita/page/67 67]|url=https://archive.org/details/onunderstandingj0000kita|url-access=registration}}</ref>
==Etymology==
There is no single universally accepted theory to explain the meaning of the term.<ref name="Philippi2">Philippi (1990). p. 2.</ref> One theory derives ''norito'' from ''noru'' ([[wikt:宣#Kanji|宣る]], 'to declare'; cf. the verbs ''[[wikt:祈る|inoru]]'' 'to pray' and ''[[wikt:呪う|norou]]'' 'to curse'<ref name="Philippi2" />) - combined with the suffix ''-to''.<ref name="EncyclopediaShinto" /> A variant term, ''notto'', is derived from a combination of ''norito'' with ''koto'', 'word'.<ref name="EncyclopediaShinto" />
There are various known ways of writing the word in [[kanji]]: aside from 祝詞 (currently the standard), 詔戸言, 詔刀言, and 諄辞 are also attested.<ref name="EncyclopediaShinto" />
One recent writer summed up the original meaning of ''norito'' as "a general term meaning [[Kotodama|magic by means of words]]."<ref>Shiraishi, Mitsukuni, cited in Philippi (1990). p. 2.</ref>
== Form and content == The Shinto religion did not produce any writings, particularly those that inferred from myths and legends, that would have constituted a religious theology except for the norito.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Sources of Japanese Tradition: From earliest times to 1600, Second Edition|last1=de Bary|first1=William Theodore|last2=Keene|first2=Donald|last3=Tanabe|first3=George|last4=Varley|first4=Paul|date=2001|publisher=Columbia University Press|isbn=0231121385|location=New York|pages=[https://archive.org/details/sourcesofjapanes00colu/page/336 336]|url=https://archive.org/details/sourcesofjapanes00colu/page/336}}</ref> (One should, however, note that the [[Kojiki]] and the [[Nihongi]], while written primarily as historical works rather than sacred scriptures, do contain mythical narratives of the Shinto tradition.) These few prayers were primarily used in purification rituals and articulated gratitude towards the gods for the blessings of ''kami'' or to ask for climate change such as rain.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Japanese Mythology in Film: A Semiotic Approach to Reading Japanese Film and Anime|last=Okuyama|first=Yoshiko|author-link=Yoshiko Okuyama|date=2015|publisher=Lexington Books|isbn=9780739190920|location=Lanham, MD|pages=87}}</ref>
Norito is a form of a rhythmic [[Poetry|poem]] recited to facilitate the transmission of posterity.<ref name=":0" /> The incantation would usually begin with praises for the supreme power of ''kami'' and concludes with an expression of respect and awe.<ref name=":0" /> The ''Nakatomi no Harae Kunge'' or the ''Exposition of the Ritual of Purification'' describes norito within a process that implies the idea of human beings as children of the ''kami'' who lost their purity but who return to their [[Divinity|divine]] origin by restoring it.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|date=2004|publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group|isbn=0313264325|location=Westport, CT|pages=84–85}}</ref>
==Reading==
''Norito'' were (and still are) traditionally written in a variety of ''[[man'yōgana]]'' where [[Japanese particles|particles]] and suffixes are written in a smaller script than the main body of the text.<ref>{{cite book|editor1-last=Sinor|editor1-first=Denis|title=American Oriental Society, Middle West Branch, Semi-Centennial Volume: A Collection of Original Essays|date=1969|publisher=Indiana University Press|pages=242–243|isbn=978-0-253-39503-0 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=vGEDAAAAMAAJ}}</ref> This style of writing, used in imperial edicts (宣命 ''senmyō'') preserved in the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' and other texts dating from the 8th century ([[Nara period]]), is known as ''senmyōgaki''.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Seeley|first1=Christopher|title=A History of Writing in Japan|date=1991|publisher=Brill|isbn=978-9004090811|pages=54–55|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KCZ2ya6cg88C&pg=PA54}}</ref>
==See also== {{wikisourcelang|ja|祓詞}} {{wikisourcelang|ja|大祓詞}} {{wikisourcelang|ja|六月晦大祓祝詞}}
*[[Kotodama]]

== Form and content == The Shinto religion did not produce any writings, particularly those that inferred from myths and legends, that would have constituted a religious theology except for the norito.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Sources of Japanese Tradition: From earliest times to 1600, Second Edition|last1=de Bary|first1=William Theodore|last2=Keene|first2=Donald|last3=Tanabe|first3=George|last4=Varley|first4=Paul|date=2001|publisher=Columbia University Press|isbn=0231121385|location=New York|pages=[https://archive.org/details/sourcesofjapanes00colu/page/336 336]|url=https://archive.org/details/sourcesofjapanes00colu/page/336}}</ref> (One should, however, note that the [[Kojiki]] and the [[Nihongi]], while written primarily as historical works rather than sacred scriptures, do contain mythical narratives of the Shinto tradition.) These few prayers were primarily used in purification rituals and articulated gratitude towards the gods for the blessings of ''kami'' or to ask for climate change such as rain.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Japanese Mythology in Film: A Semiotic Approach to Reading Japanese Film and Anime|last=Okuyama|first=Yoshiko|author-link=Yoshiko Okuyama|date=2015|publisher=Lexington Books|isbn=9780739190920|location=Lanham, MD|pages=87}}</ref>
Norito is a form of a rhythmic [[Poetry|poem]] recited to facilitate the transmission of posterity.<ref name=":0" /> The incantation would usually begin with praises for the supreme power of ''kami'' and concludes with an expression of respect and awe.<ref name=":0" /> The ''Nakatomi no Harae Kunge'' or the ''Exposition of the Ritual of Purification'' describes norito within a process that implies the idea of human beings as children of the ''kami'' who lost their purity but who return to their [[Divinity|divine]] origin by restoring it.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|date=2004|publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group|isbn=0313264325|location=Westport, CT|pages=84–85}}</ref>
==Reading==
''Norito'' were (and still are) traditionally written in a variety of ''[[man'yōgana]]'' where [[Japanese particles|particles]] and suffixes are written in a smaller script than the main body of the text.<ref>{{cite book|editor1-last=Sinor|editor1-first=Denis|title=American Oriental Society, Middle West Branch, Semi-Centennial Volume: A Collection of Original Essays|date=1969|publisher=Indiana University Press|pages=242–243|isbn=978-0-253-39503-0 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=vGEDAAAAMAAJ}}</ref> This style of writing, used in imperial edicts (宣命 ''senmyō'') preserved in the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' and other texts dating from the 8th century ([[Nara period]]), is known as ''senmyōgaki''.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Seeley|first1=Christopher|title=A History of Writing in Japan|date=1991|publisher=Brill|isbn=978-9004090811|pages=54–55|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KCZ2ya6cg88C&pg=PA54}}</ref>
==See also== {{wikisourcelang|ja|祓詞}} {{wikisourcelang|ja|大祓詞}} {{wikisourcelang|ja|六月晦大祓祝詞}}
*[[Kotodama]]

{{Short description|Liturgical texts or ritual incantations in Shinto}} {{lacking overview|date=April 2014}} {{Shinto}} {{Nihongo|'''Norito'''|祝詞}} are [[Liturgy|liturgical]] texts or ritual incantations in [[Shinto]], usually addressed to a given ''[[kami]]''.<ref>{{cite book|last=Philippi|first=Donald L.|title=Norito: A Translation of the Ancient Japanese Ritual Prayers|year=1990|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=0691014892|page=vii}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|title=Norito|url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/norito|website=Encyclopedia Britannica}}</ref><ref name="EncyclopediaShinto">{{cite web|last1=Motosawa|first1=Masafumi|title=Norito|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1246|website=Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Kokugakuin University}}</ref>
== History == The first written documentation of ''norito'' dates to 712 CE in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and 720 CE in the ''[[Nihongi]]''.<ref name="EncyclopediaShinto" />
The [[Engishiki]], a compilation of laws and minute regulation presented by the court compiled in 927 CE, preserves twenty-seven representative forms of ''norito''.<ref>Philippi (1990). p. 1.</ref><ref>{{cite book|last1=Kitagawa|first1=Joseph Mitsuo|title=On Understanding Japanese Religion|date=1987|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=978-0691102290|page=[https://archive.org/details/onunderstandingj0000kita/page/67 67]|url=https://archive.org/details/onunderstandingj0000kita|url-access=registration}}</ref>
==Etymology==
There is no single universally accepted theory to explain the meaning of the term.<ref name="Philippi2">Philippi (1990). p. 2.</ref> One theory derives ''norito'' from ''noru'' ([[wikt:宣#Kanji|宣る]], 'to declare'; cf. the verbs ''[[wikt:祈る|inoru]]'' 'to pray' and ''[[wikt:呪う|norou]]'' 'to curse'<ref name="Philippi2" />) - combined with the suffix ''-to''.<ref name="EncyclopediaShinto" /> A variant term, ''notto'', is derived from a combination of ''norito'' with ''koto'', 'word'.<ref name="EncyclopediaShinto" />
There are various known ways of writing the word in [[kanji]]: aside from 祝詞 (currently the standard), 詔戸言, 詔刀言, and 諄辞 are also attested.<ref name="EncyclopediaShinto" />
One recent writer summed up the original meaning of ''norito'' as "a general term meaning [[Kotodama|magic by means of words]]."<ref>Shiraishi, Mitsukuni, cited in Philippi (1990). p. 2.</ref>
== Form and content == The Shinto religion did not produce any writings, particularly those that inferred from myths and legends, that would have constituted a religious theology except for the norito.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Sources of Japanese Tradition: From earliest times to 1600, Second Edition|last1=de Bary|first1=William Theodore|last2=Keene|first2=Donald|last3=Tanabe|first3=George|last4=Varley|first4=Paul|date=2001|publisher=Columbia University Press|isbn=0231121385|location=New York|pages=[https://archive.org/details/sourcesofjapanes00colu/page/336 336]|url=https://archive.org/details/sourcesofjapanes00colu/page/336}}</ref> (One should, however, note that the [[Kojiki]] and the [[Nihongi]], while written primarily as historical works rather than sacred scriptures, do contain mythical narratives of the Shinto tradition.) These few prayers were primarily used in purification rituals and articulated gratitude towards the gods for the blessings of ''kami'' or to ask for climate change such as rain.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Japanese Mythology in Film: A Semiotic Approach to Reading Japanese Film and Anime|last=Okuyama|first=Yoshiko|author-link=Yoshiko Okuyama|date=2015|publisher=Lexington Books|isbn=9780739190920|location=Lanham, MD|pages=87}}</ref>
Norito is a form of a rhythmic [[Poetry|poem]] recited to facilitate the transmission of posterity.<ref name=":0" /> The incantation would usually begin with praises for the supreme power of ''kami'' and concludes with an expression of respect and awe.<ref name=":0" /> The ''Nakatomi no Harae Kunge'' or the ''Exposition of the Ritual of Purification'' describes norito within a process that implies the idea of human beings as children of the ''kami'' who lost their purity but who return to their [[Divinity|divine]] origin by restoring it.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|date=2004|publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group|isbn=0313264325|location=Westport, CT|pages=84–85}}</ref>
==Reading==
''Norito'' were (and still are) traditionally written in a variety of ''[[man'yōgana]]'' where [[Japanese particles|particles]] and suffixes are written in a smaller script than the main body of the text.<ref>{{cite book|editor1-last=Sinor|editor1-first=Denis|title=American Oriental Society, Middle West Branch, Semi-Centennial Volume: A Collection of Original Essays|date=1969|publisher=Indiana University Press|pages=242–243|isbn=978-0-253-39503-0 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=vGEDAAAAMAAJ}}</ref> This style of writing, used in imperial edicts (宣命 ''senmyō'') preserved in the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' and other texts dating from the 8th century ([[Nara period]]), is known as ''senmyōgaki''.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Seeley|first1=Christopher|title=A History of Writing in Japan|date=1991|publisher=Brill|isbn=978-9004090811|pages=54–55|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KCZ2ya6cg88C&pg=PA54}}</ref>
==See also== {{wikisourcelang|ja|祓詞}} {{wikisourcelang|ja|大祓詞}} {{wikisourcelang|ja|六月晦大祓祝詞}}
*[[Kotodama]]

{{Short description|Liturgical texts or ritual incantations in Shinto}} {{lacking overview|date=April 2014}} {{Shinto}} {{Nihongo|'''Norito'''|祝詞}} are [[Liturgy|liturgical]] texts or ritual incantations in [[Shinto]], usually addressed to a given ''[[kami]]''.<ref>{{cite book|last=Philippi|first=Donald L.|title=Norito: A Translation of the Ancient Japanese Ritual Prayers|year=1990|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=0691014892|page=vii}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|title=Norito|url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/norito|website=Encyclopedia Britannica}}</ref><ref name="EncyclopediaShinto">{{cite web|last1=Motosawa|first1=Masafumi|title=Norito|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1246|website=Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Kokugakuin University}}</ref>

There is no single universally accepted theory to explain the meaning of the term.<ref name="Philippi2">Philippi (1990). p. 2.</ref> One theory derives ''norito'' from ''noru'' ([[wikt:宣#Kanji|宣る]], 'to declare'; cf. the verbs ''[[wikt:祈る|inoru]]'' 'to pray' and ''[[wikt:呪う|norou]]'' 'to curse'<ref name="Philippi2" />) - combined with the suffix ''-to''.<ref name="EncyclopediaShinto" /> A variant term, ''notto'', is derived from a combination of ''norito'' with ''koto'', 'word'.<ref name="EncyclopediaShinto" />
There are various known ways of writing the word in [[kanji]]: aside from 祝詞 (currently the standard), 詔戸言, 詔刀言, and 諄辞 are also attested.<ref name="EncyclopediaShinto" />
One recent writer summed up the original meaning of ''norito'' as "a general term meaning [[Kotodama|magic by means of words]]."<ref>Shiraishi, Mitsukuni, cited in Philippi (1990). p. 2.</ref>
== Form and content == The Shinto religion did not produce any writings, particularly those that inferred from myths and legends, that would have constituted a religious theology except for the norito.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Sources of Japanese Tradition: From earliest times to 1600, Second Edition|last1=de Bary|first1=William Theodore|last2=Keene|first2=Donald|last3=Tanabe|first3=George|last4=Varley|first4=Paul|date=2001|publisher=Columbia University Press|isbn=0231121385|location=New York|pages=[https://archive.org/details/sourcesofjapanes00colu/page/336 336]|url=https://archive.org/details/sourcesofjapanes00colu/page/336}}</ref> (One should, however, note that the [[Kojiki]] and the [[Nihongi]], while written primarily as historical works rather than sacred scriptures, do contain mythical narratives of the Shinto tradition.) These few prayers were primarily used in purification rituals and articulated gratitude towards the gods for the blessings of ''kami'' or to ask for climate change such as rain.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Japanese Mythology in Film: A Semiotic Approach to Reading Japanese Film and Anime|last=Okuyama|first=Yoshiko|author-link=Yoshiko Okuyama|date=2015|publisher=Lexington Books|isbn=9780739190920|location=Lanham, MD|pages=87}}</ref>
Norito is a form of a rhythmic [[Poetry|poem]] recited to facilitate the transmission of posterity.<ref name=":0" /> The incantation would usually begin with praises for the supreme power of ''kami'' and concludes with an expression of respect and awe.<ref name=":0" /> The ''Nakatomi no Harae Kunge'' or the ''Exposition of the Ritual of Purification'' describes norito within a process that implies the idea of human beings as children of the ''kami'' who lost their purity but who return to their [[Divinity|divine]] origin by restoring it.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|date=2004|publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group|isbn=0313264325|location=Westport, CT|pages=84–85}}</ref>
==Reading==
''Norito'' were (and still are) traditionally written in a variety of ''[[man'yōgana]]'' where [[Japanese particles|particles]] and suffixes are written in a smaller script than the main body of the text.<ref>{{cite book|editor1-last=Sinor|editor1-first=Denis|title=American Oriental Society, Middle West Branch, Semi-Centennial Volume: A Collection of Original Essays|date=1969|publisher=Indiana University Press|pages=242–243|isbn=978-0-253-39503-0 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=vGEDAAAAMAAJ}}</ref> This style of writing, used in imperial edicts (宣命 ''senmyō'') preserved in the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' and other texts dating from the 8th century ([[Nara period]]), is known as ''senmyōgaki''.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Seeley|first1=Christopher|title=A History of Writing in Japan|date=1991|publisher=Brill|isbn=978-9004090811|pages=54–55|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KCZ2ya6cg88C&pg=PA54}}</ref>
==See also== {{wikisourcelang|ja|祓詞}} {{wikisourcelang|ja|大祓詞}} {{wikisourcelang|ja|六月晦大祓祝詞}}
*[[Kotodama]]

{{Short description|Liturgical texts or ritual incantations in Shinto}} {{lacking overview|date=April 2014}} {{Shinto}} {{Nihongo|'''Norito'''|祝詞}} are [[Liturgy|liturgical]] texts or ritual incantations in [[Shinto]], usually addressed to a given ''[[kami]]''.<ref>{{cite book|last=Philippi|first=Donald L.|title=Norito: A Translation of the Ancient Japanese Ritual Prayers|year=1990|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=0691014892|page=vii}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|title=Norito|url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/norito|website=Encyclopedia Britannica}}</ref><ref name="EncyclopediaShinto">{{cite web|last1=Motosawa|first1=Masafumi|title=Norito|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1246|website=Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Kokugakuin University}}</ref>
== History == The first written documentation of ''norito'' dates to 712 CE in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and 720 CE in the ''[[Nihongi]]''.<ref name="EncyclopediaShinto" />
The [[Engishiki]], a compilation of laws and minute regulation presented by the court compiled in 927 CE, preserves twenty-seven representative forms of ''norito''.<ref>Philippi (1990). p. 1.</ref><ref>{{cite book|last1=Kitagawa|first1=Joseph Mitsuo|title=On Understanding Japanese Religion|date=1987|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=978-0691102290|page=[https://archive.org/details/onunderstandingj0000kita/page/67 67]|url=https://archive.org/details/onunderstandingj0000kita|url-access=registration}}</ref>

One recent writer summed up the original meaning of ''norito'' as "a general term meaning [[Kotodama|magic by means of words]]."<ref>Shiraishi, Mitsukuni, cited in Philippi (1990). p. 2.</ref>
== Form and content == The Shinto religion did not produce any writings, particularly those that inferred from myths and legends, that would have constituted a religious theology except for the norito.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Sources of Japanese Tradition: From earliest times to 1600, Second Edition|last1=de Bary|first1=William Theodore|last2=Keene|first2=Donald|last3=Tanabe|first3=George|last4=Varley|first4=Paul|date=2001|publisher=Columbia University Press|isbn=0231121385|location=New York|pages=[https://archive.org/details/sourcesofjapanes00colu/page/336 336]|url=https://archive.org/details/sourcesofjapanes00colu/page/336}}</ref> (One should, however, note that the [[Kojiki]] and the [[Nihongi]], while written primarily as historical works rather than sacred scriptures, do contain mythical narratives of the Shinto tradition.) These few prayers were primarily used in purification rituals and articulated gratitude towards the gods for the blessings of ''kami'' or to ask for climate change such as rain.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Japanese Mythology in Film: A Semiotic Approach to Reading Japanese Film and Anime|last=Okuyama|first=Yoshiko|author-link=Yoshiko Okuyama|date=2015|publisher=Lexington Books|isbn=9780739190920|location=Lanham, MD|pages=87}}</ref>
Norito is a form of a rhythmic [[Poetry|poem]] recited to facilitate the transmission of posterity.<ref name=":0" /> The incantation would usually begin with praises for the supreme power of ''kami'' and concludes with an expression of respect and awe.<ref name=":0" /> The ''Nakatomi no Harae Kunge'' or the ''Exposition of the Ritual of Purification'' describes norito within a process that implies the idea of human beings as children of the ''kami'' who lost their purity but who return to their [[Divinity|divine]] origin by restoring it.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|date=2004|publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group|isbn=0313264325|location=Westport, CT|pages=84–85}}</ref>
==Reading==
''Norito'' were (and still are) traditionally written in a variety of ''[[man'yōgana]]'' where [[Japanese particles|particles]] and suffixes are written in a smaller script than the main body of the text.<ref>{{cite book|editor1-last=Sinor|editor1-first=Denis|title=American Oriental Society, Middle West Branch, Semi-Centennial Volume: A Collection of Original Essays|date=1969|publisher=Indiana University Press|pages=242–243|isbn=978-0-253-39503-0 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=vGEDAAAAMAAJ}}</ref> This style of writing, used in imperial edicts (宣命 ''senmyō'') preserved in the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' and other texts dating from the 8th century ([[Nara period]]), is known as ''senmyōgaki''.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Seeley|first1=Christopher|title=A History of Writing in Japan|date=1991|publisher=Brill|isbn=978-9004090811|pages=54–55|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KCZ2ya6cg88C&pg=PA54}}</ref>

== Form and content == The Shinto religion did not produce any writings, particularly those that inferred from myths and legends, that would have constituted a religious theology except for the norito.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Sources of Japanese Tradition: From earliest times to 1600, Second Edition|last1=de Bary|first1=William Theodore|last2=Keene|first2=Donald|last3=Tanabe|first3=George|last4=Varley|first4=Paul|date=2001|publisher=Columbia University Press|isbn=0231121385|location=New York|pages=[https://archive.org/details/sourcesofjapanes00colu/page/336 336]|url=https://archive.org/details/sourcesofjapanes00colu/page/336}}</ref> (One should, however, note that the [[Kojiki]] and the [[Nihongi]], while written primarily as historical works rather than sacred scriptures, do contain mythical narratives of the Shinto tradition.) These few prayers were primarily used in purification rituals and articulated gratitude towards the gods for the blessings of ''kami'' or to ask for climate change such as rain.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Japanese Mythology in Film: A Semiotic Approach to Reading Japanese Film and Anime|last=Okuyama|first=Yoshiko|author-link=Yoshiko Okuyama|date=2015|publisher=Lexington Books|isbn=9780739190920|location=Lanham, MD|pages=87}}</ref>
Norito is a form of a rhythmic [[Poetry|poem]] recited to facilitate the transmission of posterity.<ref name=":0" /> The incantation would usually begin with praises for the supreme power of ''kami'' and concludes with an expression of respect and awe.<ref name=":0" /> The ''Nakatomi no Harae Kunge'' or the ''Exposition of the Ritual of Purification'' describes norito within a process that implies the idea of human beings as children of the ''kami'' who lost their purity but who return to their [[Divinity|divine]] origin by restoring it.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|date=2004|publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group|isbn=0313264325|location=Westport, CT|pages=84–85}}</ref>
==Reading==
''Norito'' were (and still are) traditionally written in a variety of ''[[man'yōgana]]'' where [[Japanese particles|particles]] and suffixes are written in a smaller script than the main body of the text.<ref>{{cite book|editor1-last=Sinor|editor1-first=Denis|title=American Oriental Society, Middle West Branch, Semi-Centennial Volume: A Collection of Original Essays|date=1969|publisher=Indiana University Press|pages=242–243|isbn=978-0-253-39503-0 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=vGEDAAAAMAAJ}}</ref> This style of writing, used in imperial edicts (宣命 ''senmyō'') preserved in the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' and other texts dating from the 8th century ([[Nara period]]), is known as ''senmyōgaki''.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Seeley|first1=Christopher|title=A History of Writing in Japan|date=1991|publisher=Brill|isbn=978-9004090811|pages=54–55|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KCZ2ya6cg88C&pg=PA54}}</ref>
==See also== {{wikisourcelang|ja|祓詞}} {{wikisourcelang|ja|大祓詞}} {{wikisourcelang|ja|六月晦大祓祝詞}}
*[[Kotodama]]

{{Short description|Liturgical texts or ritual incantations in Shinto}} {{lacking overview|date=April 2014}} {{Shinto}} {{Nihongo|'''Norito'''|祝詞}} are [[Liturgy|liturgical]] texts or ritual incantations in [[Shinto]], usually addressed to a given ''[[kami]]''.<ref>{{cite book|last=Philippi|first=Donald L.|title=Norito: A Translation of the Ancient Japanese Ritual Prayers|year=1990|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=0691014892|page=vii}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|title=Norito|url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/norito|website=Encyclopedia Britannica}}</ref><ref name="EncyclopediaShinto">{{cite web|last1=Motosawa|first1=Masafumi|title=Norito|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1246|website=Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Kokugakuin University}}</ref>
== History == The first written documentation of ''norito'' dates to 712 CE in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and 720 CE in the ''[[Nihongi]]''.<ref name="EncyclopediaShinto" />
The [[Engishiki]], a compilation of laws and minute regulation presented by the court compiled in 927 CE, preserves twenty-seven representative forms of ''norito''.<ref>Philippi (1990). p. 1.</ref><ref>{{cite book|last1=Kitagawa|first1=Joseph Mitsuo|title=On Understanding Japanese Religion|date=1987|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=978-0691102290|page=[https://archive.org/details/onunderstandingj0000kita/page/67 67]|url=https://archive.org/details/onunderstandingj0000kita|url-access=registration}}</ref>
==Etymology==
There is no single universally accepted theory to explain the meaning of the term.<ref name="Philippi2">Philippi (1990). p. 2.</ref> One theory derives ''norito'' from ''noru'' ([[wikt:宣#Kanji|宣る]], 'to declare'; cf. the verbs ''[[wikt:祈る|inoru]]'' 'to pray' and ''[[wikt:呪う|norou]]'' 'to curse'<ref name="Philippi2" />) - combined with the suffix ''-to''.<ref name="EncyclopediaShinto" /> A variant term, ''notto'', is derived from a combination of ''norito'' with ''koto'', 'word'.<ref name="EncyclopediaShinto" />
There are various known ways of writing the word in [[kanji]]: aside from 祝詞 (currently the standard), 詔戸言, 詔刀言, and 諄辞 are also attested.<ref name="EncyclopediaShinto" />
One recent writer summed up the original meaning of ''norito'' as "a general term meaning [[Kotodama|magic by means of words]]."<ref>Shiraishi, Mitsukuni, cited in Philippi (1990). p. 2.</ref>
== Form and content == The Shinto religion did not produce any writings, particularly those that inferred from myths and legends, that would have constituted a religious theology except for the norito.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Sources of Japanese Tradition: From earliest times to 1600, Second Edition|last1=de Bary|first1=William Theodore|last2=Keene|first2=Donald|last3=Tanabe|first3=George|last4=Varley|first4=Paul|date=2001|publisher=Columbia University Press|isbn=0231121385|location=New York|pages=[https://archive.org/details/sourcesofjapanes00colu/page/336 336]|url=https://archive.org/details/sourcesofjapanes00colu/page/336}}</ref> (One should, however, note that the [[Kojiki]] and the [[Nihongi]], while written primarily as historical works rather than sacred scriptures, do contain mythical narratives of the Shinto tradition.) These few prayers were primarily used in purification rituals and articulated gratitude towards the gods for the blessings of ''kami'' or to ask for climate change such as rain.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Japanese Mythology in Film: A Semiotic Approach to Reading Japanese Film and Anime|last=Okuyama|first=Yoshiko|author-link=Yoshiko Okuyama|date=2015|publisher=Lexington Books|isbn=9780739190920|location=Lanham, MD|pages=87}}</ref>
Norito is a form of a rhythmic [[Poetry|poem]] recited to facilitate the transmission of posterity.<ref name=":0" /> The incantation would usually begin with praises for the supreme power of ''kami'' and concludes with an expression of respect and awe.<ref name=":0" /> The ''Nakatomi no Harae Kunge'' or the ''Exposition of the Ritual of Purification'' describes norito within a process that implies the idea of human beings as children of the ''kami'' who lost their purity but who return to their [[Divinity|divine]] origin by restoring it.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|date=2004|publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group|isbn=0313264325|location=Westport, CT|pages=84–85}}</ref>
==Reading==
''Norito'' were (and still are) traditionally written in a variety of ''[[man'yōgana]]'' where [[Japanese particles|particles]] and suffixes are written in a smaller script than the main body of the text.<ref>{{cite book|editor1-last=Sinor|editor1-first=Denis|title=American Oriental Society, Middle West Branch, Semi-Centennial Volume: A Collection of Original Essays|date=1969|publisher=Indiana University Press|pages=242–243|isbn=978-0-253-39503-0 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=vGEDAAAAMAAJ}}</ref> This style of writing, used in imperial edicts (宣命 ''senmyō'') preserved in the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' and other texts dating from the 8th century ([[Nara period]]), is known as ''senmyōgaki''.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Seeley|first1=Christopher|title=A History of Writing in Japan|date=1991|publisher=Brill|isbn=978-9004090811|pages=54–55|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KCZ2ya6cg88C&pg=PA54}}</ref>
==See also== {{wikisourcelang|ja|祓詞}} {{wikisourcelang|ja|大祓詞}} {{wikisourcelang|ja|六月晦大祓祝詞}}
*[[Kotodama]]
==References== {{Reflist}}

B.|date=2004|publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group|isbn=0313264325|location=Westport, CT|pages=84–85}}</ref>

''Norito'' were (and still are) traditionally written in a variety of ''[[man'yōgana]]'' where [[Japanese particles|particles]] and suffixes are written in a smaller script than the main body of the text.<ref>{{cite book|editor1-last=Sinor|editor1-first=Denis|title=American Oriental Society, Middle West Branch, Semi-Centennial Volume: A Collection of Original Essays|date=1969|publisher=Indiana University Press|pages=242–243|isbn=978-0-253-39503-0 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=vGEDAAAAMAAJ}}</ref> This style of writing, used in imperial edicts (宣命 ''senmyō'') preserved in the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' and other texts dating from the 8th century ([[Nara period]]), is known as ''senmyōgaki''.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Seeley|first1=Christopher|title=A History of Writing in Japan|date=1991|publisher=Brill|isbn=978-9004090811|pages=54–55|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KCZ2ya6cg88C&pg=PA54}}</ref>

==See also== {{wikisourcelang|ja|祓詞}} {{wikisourcelang|ja|大祓詞}} {{wikisourcelang|ja|六月晦大祓祝詞}}

revision.text:
{{Short description|Liturgical texts or ritual incantations in Shinto}} {{lacking overview|date=April 2014}} {{Shinto}} {{Nihongo|'''Norito'''|祝詞}} are [[Liturgy|liturgical]] texts or ritual incantations in [[Shinto]], usually addressed to a given ''[[kami]]''.<ref>{{cite book|last=Philippi|first=Donald L.|title=Norito: A Translation of the Ancient Japanese Ritual Prayers|year=1990|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=0691014892|page=vii}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|title=Norito|url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/norito|website=Encyclopedia Britannica}}</ref><ref name="EncyclopediaShinto">{{cite web|last1=Motosawa|first1=Masafumi|title=Norito|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1246|website=Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Kokugakuin University}}</ref>
== History == The first written documentation of ''norito'' dates to 712 CE in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and 720 CE in the ''[[Nihongi]]''.<ref name="EncyclopediaShinto" />
The [[Engishiki]], a compilation of laws and minute regulation presented by the court compiled in 927 CE, preserves twenty-seven representative forms of ''norito''.<ref>Philippi (1990). p. 1.</ref><ref>{{cite book|last1=Kitagawa|first1=Joseph Mitsuo|title=On Understanding Japanese Religion|date=1987|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=978-0691102290|page=[https://archive.org/details/onunderstandingj0000kita/page/67 67]|url=https://archive.org/details/onunderstandingj0000kita|url-access=registration}}</ref>
==Etymology==
There is no single universally accepted theory to explain the meaning of the term.<ref name="Philippi2">Philippi (1990). p. 2.</ref> One theory derives ''norito'' from ''noru'' (, 'to declare'; cf. the verbs '''' 'to pray' and '''' 'to curse'<ref name="Philippi2" />) - combined with the suffix ''-to''.<ref name="EncyclopediaShinto" /> A variant term, ''notto'', is derived from a combination of ''norito'' with ''koto'', 'word'.<ref name="EncyclopediaShinto" />
There are various known ways of writing the word in [[kanji]]: aside from 祝詞 (currently the standard), 詔戸言, 詔刀言, and 諄辞 are also attested.<ref name="EncyclopediaShinto" />
One recent writer summed up the original meaning of ''norito'' as "a general term meaning [[Kotodama|magic by means of words]]."<ref>Shiraishi, Mitsukuni, cited in Philippi (1990). p. 2.</ref>
== Form and content == The Shinto religion did not produce any writings, particularly those that inferred from myths and legends, that would have constituted a religious theology except for the norito.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Sources of Japanese Tradition: From earliest times to 1600, Second Edition|last1=de Bary|first1=William Theodore|last2=Keene|first2=Donald|last3=Tanabe|first3=George|last4=Varley|first4=Paul|date=2001|publisher=Columbia University Press|isbn=0231121385|location=New York|pages=[https://archive.org/details/sourcesofjapanes00colu/page/336 336]|url=https://archive.org/details/sourcesofjapanes00colu/page/336}}</ref> (One should, however, note that the [[Kojiki]] and the [[Nihongi]], while written primarily as historical works rather than sacred scriptures, do contain mythical narratives of the Shinto tradition.) These few prayers were primarily used in purification rituals and articulated gratitude towards the gods for the blessings of ''kami'' or to ask for climate change such as rain.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Japanese Mythology in Film: A Semiotic Approach to Reading Japanese Film and Anime|last=Okuyama|first=Yoshiko|author-link=Yoshiko Okuyama|date=2015|publisher=Lexington Books|isbn=9780739190920|location=Lanham, MD|pages=87}}</ref>
Norito is a form of a rhythmic [[Poetry|poem]] recited to facilitate the transmission of posterity.<ref name=":0" /> The incantation would usually begin with praises for the supreme power of ''kami'' and concludes with an expression of respect and awe.<ref name=":0" /> The ''Nakatomi no Harae Kunge'' or the ''Exposition of the Ritual of Purification'' describes norito within a process that implies the idea of human beings as children of the ''kami'' who lost their purity but who return to their [[Divinity|divine]] origin by restoring it.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|date=2004|publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group|isbn=0313264325|location=Westport, CT|pages=84–85}}</ref>
==Reading==
''Norito'' were (and still are) traditionally written in a variety of ''[[man'yōgana]]'' where [[Japanese particles|particles]] and suffixes are written in a smaller script than the main body of the text.<ref>{{cite book|editor1-last=Sinor|editor1-first=Denis|title=American Oriental Society, Middle West Branch, Semi-Centennial Volume: A Collection of Original Essays|date=1969|publisher=Indiana University Press|pages=242–243|isbn=978-0-253-39503-0 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=vGEDAAAAMAAJ}}</ref> This style of writing, used in imperial edicts (宣命 ''senmyō'') preserved in the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' and other texts dating from the 8th century ([[Nara period]]), is known as ''senmyōgaki''.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Seeley|first1=Christopher|title=A History of Writing in Japan|date=1991|publisher=Brill|isbn=978-9004090811|pages=54–55|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KCZ2ya6cg88C&pg=PA54}}</ref>
==See also== {{wikisourcelang|ja|祓詞}} {{wikisourcelang|ja|大祓詞}} {{wikisourcelang|ja|六月晦大祓祝詞}}
*[[Kotodama]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Liturgical texts or ritual incantations in Shinto}} {{lacking overview|date=April 2014}} {{Shinto}} {{Nihongo|'''Norito'''|祝詞}} are [[Liturgy|liturgical]] texts or ritual incantations in [[Shinto]], usually addressed to a given ''[[kami]]''.<ref>{{cite book|last=Philippi|first=Donald L.|title=Norito: A Translation of the Ancient Japanese Ritual Prayers|year=1990|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=0691014892|page=vii}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|title=Norito|url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/norito|website=Encyclopedia Britannica}}</ref><ref name="EncyclopediaShinto">{{cite web|last1=Motosawa|first1=Masafumi|title=Norito|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1246|website=Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Kokugakuin University}}</ref>
== History == The first written documentation of ''norito'' dates to 712 CE in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and 720 CE in the ''[[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]]''.<ref name="EncyclopediaShinto" />
The [[Engishiki]], a compilation of laws and minute regulation presented by the court compiled in 927 CE, preserves twenty-seven representative forms of ''norito''.<ref>Philippi (1990). p. 1.</ref><ref>{{cite book|last1=Kitagawa|first1=Joseph Mitsuo|title=On Understanding Japanese Religion|date=1987|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=978-0691102290|page=[https://archive.org/details/onunderstandingj0000kita/page/67 67]|url=https://archive.org/details/onunderstandingj0000kita|url-access=registration}}</ref>
==Etymology==
There is no single universally accepted theory to explain the meaning of the term.<ref name="Philippi2">Philippi (1990). p. 2.</ref> One theory derives ''norito'' from ''noru'' (, 'to declare'; cf. the verbs '''' 'to pray' and '''' 'to curse'<ref name="Philippi2" />) - combined with the suffix ''-to''.<ref name="EncyclopediaShinto" /> A variant term, ''notto'', is derived from a combination of ''norito'' with ''koto'', 'word'.<ref name="EncyclopediaShinto" />
There are various known ways of writing the word in [[kanji]]: aside from 祝詞 (currently the standard), 詔戸言, 詔刀言, and 諄辞 are also attested.<ref name="EncyclopediaShinto" />
One recent writer summed up the original meaning of ''norito'' as "a general term meaning [[Kotodama|magic by means of words]]."<ref>Shiraishi, Mitsukuni, cited in Philippi (1990). p. 2.</ref>
== Form and content == The Shinto religion did not produce any writings, particularly those that inferred from myths and legends, that would have constituted a religious theology except for the norito.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Sources of Japanese Tradition: From earliest times to 1600, Second Edition|last1=de Bary|first1=William Theodore|last2=Keene|first2=Donald|last3=Tanabe|first3=George|last4=Varley|first4=Paul|date=2001|publisher=Columbia University Press|isbn=0231121385|location=New York|pages=[https://archive.org/details/sourcesofjapanes00colu/page/336 336]|url=https://archive.org/details/sourcesofjapanes00colu/page/336}}</ref> (One should, however, note that the [[Kojiki]] and the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]], while written primarily as historical works rather than sacred scriptures, do contain mythical narratives of the Shinto tradition.) These few prayers were primarily used in purification rituals and articulated gratitude towards the gods for the blessings of ''kami'' or to ask for climate change such as rain.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Japanese Mythology in Film: A Semiotic Approach to Reading Japanese Film and Anime|last=Okuyama|first=Yoshiko|author-link=Yoshiko Okuyama|date=2015|publisher=Lexington Books|isbn=9780739190920|location=Lanham, MD|pages=87}}</ref>
Norito is a form of a rhythmic [[Poetry|poem]] recited to facilitate the transmission of posterity.<ref name=":0" /> The incantation would usually begin with praises for the supreme power of ''kami'' and concludes with an expression of respect and awe.<ref name=":0" /> The ''Nakatomi no Harae Kunge'' or the ''Exposition of the Ritual of Purification'' describes norito within a process that implies the idea of human beings as children of the ''kami'' who lost their purity but who return to their [[Divinity|divine]] origin by restoring it.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|date=2004|publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group|isbn=0313264325|location=Westport, CT|pages=84–85}}</ref>
==Reading==
''Norito'' were (and still are) traditionally written in a variety of ''[[man'yōgana]]'' where [[Japanese particles|particles]] and suffixes are written in a smaller script than the main body of the text.<ref>{{cite book|editor1-last=Sinor|editor1-first=Denis|title=American Oriental Society, Middle West Branch, Semi-Centennial Volume: A Collection of Original Essays|date=1969|publisher=Indiana University Press|pages=242–243|isbn=978-0-253-39503-0 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=vGEDAAAAMAAJ}}</ref> This style of writing, used in imperial edicts (宣命 ''senmyō'') preserved in the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' and other texts dating from the 8th century ([[Nara period]]), is known as ''senmyōgaki''.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Seeley|first1=Christopher|title=A History of Writing in Japan|date=1991|publisher=Brill|isbn=978-9004090811|pages=54–55|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KCZ2ya6cg88C&pg=PA54}}</ref>
==See also== {{wikisourcelang|ja|祓詞}} {{wikisourcelang|ja|大祓詞}} {{wikisourcelang|ja|六月晦大祓祝詞}}
*[[Kotodama]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]]
[[Category:Shinto terminology]] [[Category:Language and mysticism]] [[Category:Shinto]]
[[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }}
[[wikt:宣#Kanji|宣る]] [[wikt:祈る|inoru]] [[wikt:呪う|norou]] [[sn:Norito]] [[en:Norito]]
[[Category:Pages without translation templates]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Norito}} [[de:Norito]] [[es:Norito]] [[fa:نوریتو]] [[fr:Norito]] [[gl:Norito]] [[id:Norito]] [[it:Norito]] [[ja:祝詞]] [[pl:Norito]] [[ru:Норито]] [[te:నోరిటో]] [[tr:Norito]] [[uk:Норіто]] [[zh:祝詞]]

{{Short description|Liturgical texts or ritual incantations in Shinto}} {{lacking overview|date=April 2014}} {{Shinto}} {{Nihongo|'''Norito'''|祝詞}} are [[Liturgy|liturgical]] texts or ritual incantations in [[Shinto]], usually addressed to a given ''[[kami]]''.<ref>{{cite book|last=Philippi|first=Donald L.|title=Norito: A Translation of the Ancient Japanese Ritual Prayers|year=1990|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=0691014892|page=vii}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|title=Norito|url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/norito|website=Encyclopedia Britannica}}</ref><ref name="EncyclopediaShinto">{{cite web|last1=Motosawa|first1=Masafumi|title=Norito|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1246|website=Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Kokugakuin University}}</ref>
== History == The first written documentation of ''norito'' dates to 712 CE in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and 720 CE in the ''[[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]]''.<ref name="EncyclopediaShinto" />
The [[Engishiki]], a compilation of laws and minute regulation presented by the court compiled in 927 CE, preserves twenty-seven representative forms of ''norito''.<ref>Philippi (1990). p. 1.</ref><ref>{{cite book|last1=Kitagawa|first1=Joseph Mitsuo|title=On Understanding Japanese Religion|date=1987|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=978-0691102290|page=[https://archive.org/details/onunderstandingj0000kita/page/67 67]|url=https://archive.org/details/onunderstandingj0000kita|url-access=registration}}</ref>
==Etymology==
There is no single universally accepted theory to explain the meaning of the term.<ref name="Philippi2">Philippi (1990). p. 2.</ref> One theory derives ''norito'' from ''noru'' (, 'to declare'; cf. the verbs '''' 'to pray' and '''' 'to curse'<ref name="Philippi2" />) - combined with the suffix ''-to''.<ref name="EncyclopediaShinto" /> A variant term, ''notto'', is derived from a combination of ''norito'' with ''koto'', 'word'.<ref name="EncyclopediaShinto" />
There are various known ways of writing the word in [[kanji]]: aside from 祝詞 (currently the standard), 詔戸言, 詔刀言, and 諄辞 are also attested.<ref name="EncyclopediaShinto" />
One recent writer summed up the original meaning of ''norito'' as "a general term meaning [[Kotodama|magic by means of words]]."<ref>Shiraishi, Mitsukuni, cited in Philippi (1990). p. 2.</ref>
== Form and content == The Shinto religion did not produce any writings, particularly those that inferred from myths and legends, that would have constituted a religious theology except for the norito.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Sources of Japanese Tradition: From earliest times to 1600, Second Edition|last1=de Bary|first1=William Theodore|last2=Keene|first2=Donald|last3=Tanabe|first3=George|last4=Varley|first4=Paul|date=2001|publisher=Columbia University Press|isbn=0231121385|location=New York|pages=[https://archive.org/details/sourcesofjapanes00colu/page/336 336]|url=https://archive.org/details/sourcesofjapanes00colu/page/336}}</ref> (One should, however, note that the [[Kojiki]] and the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]], while written primarily as historical works rather than sacred scriptures, do contain mythical narratives of the Shinto tradition.) These few prayers were primarily used in purification rituals and articulated gratitude towards the gods for the blessings of ''kami'' or to ask for climate change such as rain.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Japanese Mythology in Film: A Semiotic Approach to Reading Japanese Film and Anime|last=Okuyama|first=Yoshiko|author-link=Yoshiko Okuyama|date=2015|publisher=Lexington Books|isbn=9780739190920|location=Lanham, MD|pages=87}}</ref>
Norito is a form of a rhythmic [[Poetry|poem]] recited to facilitate the transmission of posterity.<ref name=":0" /> The incantation would usually begin with praises for the supreme power of ''kami'' and concludes with an expression of respect and awe.<ref name=":0" /> The ''Nakatomi no Harae Kunge'' or the ''Exposition of the Ritual of Purification'' describes norito within a process that implies the idea of human beings as children of the ''kami'' who lost their purity but who return to their [[:en:Divinity|divine]] origin by restoring it.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|date=2004|publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group|isbn=0313264325|location=Westport, CT|pages=84–85}}</ref>
==Reading==
''Norito'' were (and still are) traditionally written in a variety of ''[[man'yōgana]]'' where [[Japanese particles|particles]] and suffixes are written in a smaller script than the main body of the text.<ref>{{cite book|editor1-last=Sinor|editor1-first=Denis|title=American Oriental Society, Middle West Branch, Semi-Centennial Volume: A Collection of Original Essays|date=1969|publisher=Indiana University Press|pages=242–243|isbn=978-0-253-39503-0 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=vGEDAAAAMAAJ}}</ref> This style of writing, used in imperial edicts (宣命 ''senmyō'') preserved in the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' and other texts dating from the 8th century ([[Nara period]]), is known as ''senmyōgaki''.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Seeley|first1=Christopher|title=A History of Writing in Japan|date=1991|publisher=Brill|isbn=978-9004090811|pages=54–55|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KCZ2ya6cg88C&pg=PA54}}</ref>
==See also== {{wikisourcelang|ja|祓詞}} {{wikisourcelang|ja|大祓詞}} {{wikisourcelang|ja|六月晦大祓祝詞}}
*[[Kotodama]]

}}

These few prayers were primarily used in purification rituals and articulated gratitude towards the gods for the blessings of ''kami'' or to ask for climate change such as rain.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Japanese Mythology in Film: A Semiotic Approach to Reading Japanese Film and Anime|last=Okuyama|first=Yoshiko|author-link=Yoshiko Okuyama|date=2015|publisher=Lexington Books|isbn=9780739190920|location=Lanham, MD|pages=87}}</ref>

B.|date=2004|publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group|isbn=0313264325|location=Westport, CT|pages=84–85}}</ref>

{{Short description|Liturgical texts or ritual incantations in Shinto}} {{lacking overview|date=April 2014}} {{Shinto}} {{Nihongo|'''Norito'''|祝詞}} are [[Liturgy|liturgical]] texts or ritual incantations in [[Shinto]], usually addressed to a given ''[[kami]]''.<ref>{{cite book|last=Philippi|first=Donald L.|title=Norito: A Translation of the Ancient Japanese Ritual Prayers|year=1990|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=0691014892|page=vii}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|title=Norito|url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/norito|website=Encyclopedia Britannica}}</ref><ref name="EncyclopediaShinto">{{cite web|last1=Motosawa|first1=Masafumi|title=Norito|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1246|website=Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Kokugakuin University}}</ref>
== History == The first written documentation of ''norito'' dates to 712 CE in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and 720 CE in the ''[[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]]''.<ref name="EncyclopediaShinto" />
The [[Engishiki]], a compilation of laws and minute regulation presented by the court compiled in 927 CE, preserves twenty-seven representative forms of ''norito''.<ref>Philippi (1990). p. 1.</ref><ref>{{cite book|last1=Kitagawa|first1=Joseph Mitsuo|title=On Understanding Japanese Religion|date=1987|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=978-0691102290|page=[https://archive.org/details/onunderstandingj0000kita/page/67 67]|url=https://archive.org/details/onunderstandingj0000kita|url-access=registration}}</ref>
==Etymology==
There is no single universally accepted theory to explain the meaning of the term.<ref name="Philippi2">Philippi (1990). p. 2.</ref> One theory derives ''norito'' from ''noru'' (, 'to declare'; cf. the verbs '''' 'to pray' and '''' 'to curse'<ref name="Philippi2" />) - combined with the suffix ''-to''.<ref name="EncyclopediaShinto" /> A variant term, ''notto'', is derived from a combination of ''norito'' with ''koto'', 'word'.<ref name="EncyclopediaShinto" />
There are various known ways of writing the word in [[:en:Kanji|Kanji]]: aside from 祝詞 (currently the standard), 詔戸言, 詔刀言, and 諄辞 are also attested.<ref name="EncyclopediaShinto" />
One recent writer summed up the original meaning of ''norito'' as "a general term meaning [[Kotodama|magic by means of words]]."<ref>Shiraishi, Mitsukuni, cited in Philippi (1990). p. 2.</ref>
== Form and content == The Shinto religion did not produce any writings, particularly those that inferred from myths and legends, that would have constituted a religious theology except for the norito.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Sources of Japanese Tradition: From earliest times to 1600, Second Edition|last1=de Bary|first1=William Theodore|last2=Keene|first2=Donald|last3=Tanabe|first3=George|last4=Varley|first4=Paul|date=2001|publisher=Columbia University Press|isbn=0231121385|location=New York|pages=[https://archive.org/details/sourcesofjapanes00colu/page/336 336]|url=https://archive.org/details/sourcesofjapanes00colu/page/336}}</ref> (One should, however, note that the [[Kojiki]] and the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]], while written primarily as historical works rather than sacred scriptures, do contain mythical narratives of the Shinto tradition.) These few prayers were primarily used in purification rituals and articulated gratitude towards the gods for the blessings of ''kami'' or to ask for climate change such as rain.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Japanese Mythology in Film: A Semiotic Approach to Reading Japanese Film and Anime|last=Okuyama|first=Yoshiko|author-link=Yoshiko Okuyama|date=2015|publisher=Lexington Books|isbn=9780739190920|location=Lanham, MD|pages=87}}</ref>
Norito is a form of a rhythmic [[Poetry|poem]] recited to facilitate the transmission of posterity.<ref name=":0" /> The incantation would usually begin with praises for the supreme power of ''kami'' and concludes with an expression of respect and awe.<ref name=":0" /> The ''Nakatomi no Harae Kunge'' or the ''Exposition of the Ritual of Purification'' describes norito within a process that implies the idea of human beings as children of the ''kami'' who lost their purity but who return to their [[:en:Divinity|divine]] origin by restoring it.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|date=2004|publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group|isbn=0313264325|location=Westport, CT|pages=84–85}}</ref>
==Reading==
''Norito'' were (and still are) traditionally written in a variety of ''[[man'yōgana]]'' where [[:en:Japanese particles|particles]] and suffixes are written in a smaller script than the main body of the text.<ref>{{cite book|editor1-last=Sinor|editor1-first=Denis|title=American Oriental Society, Middle West Branch, Semi-Centennial Volume: A Collection of Original Essays|date=1969|publisher=Indiana University Press|pages=242–243|isbn=978-0-253-39503-0 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=vGEDAAAAMAAJ}}</ref> This style of writing, used in imperial edicts (宣命 ''senmyō'') preserved in the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' and other texts dating from the 8th century ([[Nara period]]), is known as ''senmyōgaki''.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Seeley|first1=Christopher|title=A History of Writing in Japan|date=1991|publisher=Brill|isbn=978-9004090811|pages=54–55|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KCZ2ya6cg88C&pg=PA54}}</ref>
==See also== {{wikisourcelang|ja|祓詞}} {{wikisourcelang|ja|大祓詞}} {{wikisourcelang|ja|六月晦大祓祝詞}}
*[[Kotodama]]

{{Short description|Liturgical texts or ritual incantations in Shinto}} {{lacking overview|date=April 2014}} {{Shinto}} {{Nihongo|'''Norito'''|祝詞}} are [[:en:Liturgy|liturgical]] texts or ritual incantations in [[Shinto]], usually addressed to a given ''[[kami]]''.<ref>{{cite book|last=Philippi|first=Donald L.|title=Norito: A Translation of the Ancient Japanese Ritual Prayers|year=1990|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=0691014892|page=vii}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|title=Norito|url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/norito|website=Encyclopedia Britannica}}</ref><ref name="EncyclopediaShinto">{{cite web|last1=Motosawa|first1=Masafumi|title=Norito|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1246|website=Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Kokugakuin University}}</ref>
== History == The first written documentation of ''norito'' dates to 712 CE in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and 720 CE in the ''[[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]]''.<ref name="EncyclopediaShinto" />
The [[Engishiki]], a compilation of laws and minute regulation presented by the court compiled in 927 CE, preserves twenty-seven representative forms of ''norito''.<ref>Philippi (1990). p. 1.</ref><ref>{{cite book|last1=Kitagawa|first1=Joseph Mitsuo|title=On Understanding Japanese Religion|date=1987|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=978-0691102290|page=[https://archive.org/details/onunderstandingj0000kita/page/67 67]|url=https://archive.org/details/onunderstandingj0000kita|url-access=registration}}</ref>
==Etymology==
There is no single universally accepted theory to explain the meaning of the term.<ref name="Philippi2">Philippi (1990). p. 2.</ref> One theory derives ''norito'' from ''noru'' (, 'to declare'; cf. the verbs '''' 'to pray' and '''' 'to curse'<ref name="Philippi2" />) - combined with the suffix ''-to''.<ref name="EncyclopediaShinto" /> A variant term, ''notto'', is derived from a combination of ''norito'' with ''koto'', 'word'.<ref name="EncyclopediaShinto" />
There are various known ways of writing the word in [[:en:Kanji|Kanji]]: aside from 祝詞 (currently the standard), 詔戸言, 詔刀言, and 諄辞 are also attested.<ref name="EncyclopediaShinto" />
One recent writer summed up the original meaning of ''norito'' as "a general term meaning [[Kotodama|magic by means of words]]."<ref>Shiraishi, Mitsukuni, cited in Philippi (1990). p. 2.</ref>
== Form and content == The Shinto religion did not produce any writings, particularly those that inferred from myths and legends, that would have constituted a religious theology except for the norito.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Sources of Japanese Tradition: From earliest times to 1600, Second Edition|last1=de Bary|first1=William Theodore|last2=Keene|first2=Donald|last3=Tanabe|first3=George|last4=Varley|first4=Paul|date=2001|publisher=Columbia University Press|isbn=0231121385|location=New York|pages=[https://archive.org/details/sourcesofjapanes00colu/page/336 336]|url=https://archive.org/details/sourcesofjapanes00colu/page/336}}</ref> (One should, however, note that the [[Kojiki]] and the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]], while written primarily as historical works rather than sacred scriptures, do contain mythical narratives of the Shinto tradition.) These few prayers were primarily used in purification rituals and articulated gratitude towards the gods for the blessings of ''kami'' or to ask for climate change such as rain.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Japanese Mythology in Film: A Semiotic Approach to Reading Japanese Film and Anime|last=Okuyama|first=Yoshiko|author-link=Yoshiko Okuyama|date=2015|publisher=Lexington Books|isbn=9780739190920|location=Lanham, MD|pages=87}}</ref>
Norito is a form of a rhythmic [[Poetry|poem]] recited to facilitate the transmission of posterity.<ref name=":0" /> The incantation would usually begin with praises for the supreme power of ''kami'' and concludes with an expression of respect and awe.<ref name=":0" /> The ''Nakatomi no Harae Kunge'' or the ''Exposition of the Ritual of Purification'' describes norito within a process that implies the idea of human beings as children of the ''kami'' who lost their purity but who return to their [[:en:Divinity|divine]] origin by restoring it.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|date=2004|publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group|isbn=0313264325|location=Westport, CT|pages=84–85}}</ref>
==Reading==
''Norito'' were (and still are) traditionally written in a variety of ''[[:en:Man'yōgana|Man'yōgana]]'' where [[:en:Japanese particles|particles]] and suffixes are written in a smaller script than the main body of the text.<ref>{{cite book|editor1-last=Sinor|editor1-first=Denis|title=American Oriental Society, Middle West Branch, Semi-Centennial Volume: A Collection of Original Essays|date=1969|publisher=Indiana University Press|pages=242–243|isbn=978-0-253-39503-0 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=vGEDAAAAMAAJ}}</ref> This style of writing, used in imperial edicts (宣命 ''senmyō'') preserved in the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' and other texts dating from the 8th century ([[:en:Nara period|Nara period]]), is known as ''senmyōgaki''.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Seeley|first1=Christopher|title=A History of Writing in Japan|date=1991|publisher=Brill|isbn=978-9004090811|pages=54–55|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KCZ2ya6cg88C&pg=PA54}}</ref>
==See also== {{wikisourcelang|ja|祓詞}} {{wikisourcelang|ja|大祓詞}} {{wikisourcelang|ja|六月晦大祓祝詞}}
*[[Kotodama]]
==References== {{Reflist}}

[[Category:Shinto terminology]] [[Category:Language and mysticism]] [[Category:Shinto]]
}}

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q1760119]] Wikidata name: norito [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Liturgical texts or ritual incantations in Shinto}} {{lacking overview|date=April 2014}} {{Shinto}} {{Nihongo|'''Norito'''|祝詞}} are [[:en:Liturgy|liturgical]] texts or ritual incantations in [[Shinto]], usually addressed to a given ''[[kami]]''.<ref>{{cite book|last=Philippi|first=Donald L.|title=Norito: A Translation of the Ancient Japanese Ritual Prayers|year=1990|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=0691014892|page=vii}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|title=Norito|url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/norito|website=Encyclopedia Britannica}}</ref><ref name="EncyclopediaShinto">{{cite web|last1=Motosawa|first1=Masafumi|title=Norito|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1246|website=Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Kokugakuin University}}</ref>
== History == The first written documentation of ''norito'' dates to 712 CE in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and 720 CE in the ''[[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]]''.<ref name="EncyclopediaShinto" />
The [[Engishiki]], a compilation of laws and minute regulation presented by the court compiled in 927 CE, preserves twenty-seven representative forms of ''norito''.<ref>Philippi (1990). p. 1.</ref><ref>{{cite book|last1=Kitagawa|first1=Joseph Mitsuo|title=On Understanding Japanese Religion|date=1987|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=978-0691102290|page=[https://archive.org/details/onunderstandingj0000kita/page/67 67]|url=https://archive.org/details/onunderstandingj0000kita|url-access=registration}}</ref>
==Etymology==
There is no single universally accepted theory to explain the meaning of the term.<ref name="Philippi2">Philippi (1990). p. 2.</ref> One theory derives ''norito'' from ''noru'' (, 'to declare'; cf. the verbs '''' 'to pray' and '''' 'to curse'<ref name="Philippi2" />) - combined with the suffix ''-to''.<ref name="EncyclopediaShinto" /> A variant term, ''notto'', is derived from a combination of ''norito'' with ''koto'', 'word'.<ref name="EncyclopediaShinto" />
There are various known ways of writing the word in [[:en:Kanji|Kanji]]: aside from 祝詞 (currently the standard), 詔戸言, 詔刀言, and 諄辞 are also attested.<ref name="EncyclopediaShinto" />
One recent writer summed up the original meaning of ''norito'' as "a general term meaning [[Kotodama|magic by means of words]]."<ref>Shiraishi, Mitsukuni, cited in Philippi (1990). p. 2.</ref>
== Form and content == The Shinto religion did not produce any writings, particularly those that inferred from myths and legends, that would have constituted a religious theology except for the norito.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Sources of Japanese Tradition: From earliest times to 1600, Second Edition|last1=de Bary|first1=William Theodore|last2=Keene|first2=Donald|last3=Tanabe|first3=George|last4=Varley|first4=Paul|date=2001|publisher=Columbia University Press|isbn=0231121385|location=New York|pages=[https://archive.org/details/sourcesofjapanes00colu/page/336 336]|url=https://archive.org/details/sourcesofjapanes00colu/page/336}}</ref> (One should, however, note that the [[Kojiki]] and the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]], while written primarily as historical works rather than sacred scriptures, do contain mythical narratives of the Shinto tradition.) These few prayers were primarily used in purification rituals and articulated gratitude towards the gods for the blessings of ''kami'' or to ask for climate change such as rain.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Japanese Mythology in Film: A Semiotic Approach to Reading Japanese Film and Anime|last=Okuyama|first=Yoshiko|author-link=Yoshiko Okuyama|date=2015|publisher=Lexington Books|isbn=9780739190920|location=Lanham, MD|pages=87}}</ref>
Norito is a form of a rhythmic [[:en:Poetry|poem]] recited to facilitate the transmission of posterity.<ref name=":0" /> The incantation would usually begin with praises for the supreme power of ''kami'' and concludes with an expression of respect and awe.<ref name=":0" /> The ''Nakatomi no Harae Kunge'' or the ''Exposition of the Ritual of Purification'' describes norito within a process that implies the idea of human beings as children of the ''kami'' who lost their purity but who return to their [[:en:Divinity|divine]] origin by restoring it.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|date=2004|publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group|isbn=0313264325|location=Westport, CT|pages=84–85}}</ref>
==Reading==
''Norito'' were (and still are) traditionally written in a variety of ''[[:en:Man'yōgana|Man'yōgana]]'' where [[:en:Japanese particles|particles]] and suffixes are written in a smaller script than the main body of the text.<ref>{{cite book|editor1-last=Sinor|editor1-first=Denis|title=American Oriental Society, Middle West Branch, Semi-Centennial Volume: A Collection of Original Essays|date=1969|publisher=Indiana University Press|pages=242–243|isbn=978-0-253-39503-0 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=vGEDAAAAMAAJ}}</ref> This style of writing, used in imperial edicts (宣命 ''senmyō'') preserved in the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' and other texts dating from the 8th century ([[:en:Nara period|Nara period]]), is known as ''senmyōgaki''.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Seeley|first1=Christopher|title=A History of Writing in Japan|date=1991|publisher=Brill|isbn=978-9004090811|pages=54–55|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KCZ2ya6cg88C&pg=PA54}}</ref>
==See also== {{wikisourcelang|ja|祓詞}} {{wikisourcelang|ja|大祓詞}} {{wikisourcelang|ja|六月晦大祓祝詞}}
*[[Kotodama]]

}}

B.|date=2004|publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group|isbn=0313264325|location=Westport, CT|pages=84–85}}</ref>

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Norito]][[de:Norito]][[en:Norito]][[es:Norito]][[fa:نوریتو]][[fr:Norito]][[gl:Norito]][[id:Norito]][[it:Norito]][[ja:祝詞]][[pl:Norito]][[ru:Норито]][[te:నోరిటో]][[tr:Norito]][[uk:Норіто]][[zh:祝詞]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1760119]] Wikidata name: norito [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Liturgical texts or ritual incantations in Shinto}} {{lacking overview|date=April 2014}} {{Shinto}} {{Nihongo|'''Norito'''|祝詞}} are [[:en:Liturgy|liturgical]] texts or ritual incantations in [[Shinto]], usually addressed to a given ''[[kami]]''.<ref>{{cite book|last=Philippi|first=Donald L.|title=Norito: A Translation of the Ancient Japanese Ritual Prayers|year=1990|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=0691014892|page=vii}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|title=Norito|url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/norito|website=Encyclopedia Britannica}}</ref><ref name="EncyclopediaShinto">{{cite web|last1=Motosawa|first1=Masafumi|title=Norito|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1246|website=Encyclopedia of Shinto|publisher=Kokugakuin University}}</ref>
== History == The first written documentation of ''norito'' dates to 712 CE in the ''[[Kojiki]]'' and 720 CE in the ''[[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]]''.<ref name="EncyclopediaShinto" />
The [[Engishiki]], a compilation of laws and minute regulation presented by the court compiled in 927 CE, preserves twenty-seven representative forms of ''norito''.<ref>Philippi (1990). p. 1.</ref><ref>{{cite book|last1=Kitagawa|first1=Joseph Mitsuo|title=On Understanding Japanese Religion|date=1987|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=978-0691102290|page=[https://archive.org/details/onunderstandingj0000kita/page/67 67]|url=https://archive.org/details/onunderstandingj0000kita|url-access=registration}}</ref>
==Etymology==
There is no single universally accepted theory to explain the meaning of the term.<ref name="Philippi2">Philippi (1990). p. 2.</ref> One theory derives ''norito'' from ''noru'' (, 'to declare'; cf. the verbs '''' 'to pray' and '''' 'to curse'<ref name="Philippi2" />) - combined with the suffix ''-to''.<ref name="EncyclopediaShinto" /> A variant term, ''notto'', is derived from a combination of ''norito'' with ''koto'', 'word'.<ref name="EncyclopediaShinto" />
There are various known ways of writing the word in [[:en:Kanji|Kanji]]: aside from 祝詞 (currently the standard), 詔戸言, 詔刀言, and 諄辞 are also attested.<ref name="EncyclopediaShinto" />
One recent writer summed up the original meaning of ''norito'' as "a general term meaning [[Kotodama|magic by means of words]]."<ref>Shiraishi, Mitsukuni, cited in Philippi (1990). p. 2.</ref>
== Form and content == The Shinto religion did not produce any writings, particularly those that inferred from myths and legends, that would have constituted a religious theology except for the norito.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Sources of Japanese Tradition: From earliest times to 1600, Second Edition|last1=de Bary|first1=William Theodore|last2=Keene|first2=Donald|last3=Tanabe|first3=George|last4=Varley|first4=Paul|date=2001|publisher=Columbia University Press|isbn=0231121385|location=New York|pages=[https://archive.org/details/sourcesofjapanes00colu/page/336 336]|url=https://archive.org/details/sourcesofjapanes00colu/page/336}}</ref> (One should, however, note that the [[Kojiki]] and the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]], while written primarily as historical works rather than sacred scriptures, do contain mythical narratives of the Shinto tradition.) These few prayers were primarily used in purification rituals and articulated gratitude towards the gods for the blessings of ''kami'' or to ask for climate change such as rain.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Japanese Mythology in Film: A Semiotic Approach to Reading Japanese Film and Anime|last=Okuyama|first=Yoshiko|author-link=Yoshiko Okuyama|date=2015|publisher=Lexington Books|isbn=9780739190920|location=Lanham, MD|pages=87}}</ref>
Norito is a form of a rhythmic [[:en:Poetry|poem]] recited to facilitate the transmission of posterity.<ref name=":0" /> The incantation would usually begin with praises for the supreme power of ''kami'' and concludes with an expression of respect and awe.<ref name=":0" /> The ''Nakatomi no Harae Kunge'' or the ''Exposition of the Ritual of Purification'' describes norito within a process that implies the idea of human beings as children of the ''kami'' who lost their purity but who return to their [[:en:Divinity|divine]] origin by restoring it.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|date=2004|publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group|isbn=0313264325|location=Westport, CT|pages=84–85}}</ref>
==Reading==
''Norito'' were (and still are) traditionally written in a variety of ''[[:en:Man'yōgana|Man'yōgana]]'' where [[:en:Japanese particles|particles]] and suffixes are written in a smaller script than the main body of the text.<ref>{{cite book|editor1-last=Sinor|editor1-first=Denis|title=American Oriental Society, Middle West Branch, Semi-Centennial Volume: A Collection of Original Essays|date=1969|publisher=Indiana University Press|pages=242–243|isbn=978-0-253-39503-0 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=vGEDAAAAMAAJ}}</ref> This style of writing, used in imperial edicts (宣命 ''senmyō'') preserved in the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' and other texts dating from the 8th century ([[:en:Nara period|Nara period]]), is known as ''senmyōgaki''.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Seeley|first1=Christopher|title=A History of Writing in Japan|date=1991|publisher=Brill|isbn=978-9004090811|pages=54–55|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KCZ2ya6cg88C&pg=PA54}}</ref>
==See also== {{wikisourcelang|ja|祓詞}} {{wikisourcelang|ja|大祓詞}} {{wikisourcelang|ja|六月晦大祓祝詞}}
*[[Kotodama]]

These few prayers were primarily used in purification rituals and articulated gratitude towards the gods for the blessings of ''kami'' or to ask for climate change such as rain.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Japanese Mythology in Film: A Semiotic Approach to Reading Japanese Film and Anime|last=Okuyama|first=Yoshiko|author-link=Yoshiko Okuyama|date=2015|publisher=Lexington Books|isbn=9780739190920|location=Lanham, MD|pages=87}}</ref>

B.|date=2004|publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group|isbn=0313264325|location=Westport, CT|pages=84–85}}</ref>

There are various known ways of writing the word in [[:en:Kanji|Kanji]]: aside from 祝詞 (currently the standard), 詔戸言, 詔刀言, and 諄辞 are also attested.<ref name="EncyclopediaShinto" />
One recent writer summed up the original meaning of ''norito'' as "a general term meaning [[Kotodama|magic by means of words]]."<ref>Shiraishi, Mitsukuni, cited in Philippi (1990). p. 2.</ref>
== Form and content == The Shinto religion did not produce any writings, particularly those that inferred from myths and legends, that would have constituted a religious theology except for the norito.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Sources of Japanese Tradition: From earliest times to 1600, Second Edition|last1=de Bary|first1=William Theodore|last2=Keene|first2=Donald|last3=Tanabe|first3=George|last4=Varley|first4=Paul|date=2001|publisher=Columbia University Press|isbn=0231121385|location=New York|pages=[https://archive.org/details/sourcesofjapanes00colu/page/336 336]|url=https://archive.org/details/sourcesofjapanes00colu/page/336}}</ref> (One should, however, note that the [[Kojiki]] and the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihongi]], while written primarily as historical works rather than sacred scriptures, do contain mythical narratives of the Shinto tradition.) These few prayers were primarily used in purification rituals and articulated gratitude towards the gods for the blessings of ''kami'' or to ask for climate change such as rain.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=Japanese Mythology in Film: A Semiotic Approach to Reading Japanese Film and Anime|last=Okuyama|first=Yoshiko|author-link=Yoshiko Okuyama|date=2015|publisher=Lexington Books|isbn=9780739190920|location=Lanham, MD|pages=87}}</ref>
Norito is a form of a rhythmic [[:en:Poetry|poem]] recited to facilitate the transmission of posterity.<ref name=":0" /> The incantation would usually begin with praises for the supreme power of ''kami'' and concludes with an expression of respect and awe.<ref name=":0" /> The ''Nakatomi no Harae Kunge'' or the ''Exposition of the Ritual of Purification'' describes norito within a process that implies the idea of human beings as children of the ''kami'' who lost their purity but who return to their [[:en:Divinity|divine]] origin by restoring it.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|date=2004|publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group|isbn=0313264325|location=Westport, CT|pages=84–85}}</ref>
==Reading==
''Norito'' were (and still are) traditionally written in a variety of ''[[:en:Man'yōgana|Man'yōgana]]'' where [[:en:Japanese particles|particles]] and suffixes are written in a smaller script than the main body of the text.<ref>{{cite book|editor1-last=Sinor|editor1-first=Denis|title=American Oriental Society, Middle West Branch, Semi-Centennial Volume: A Collection of Original Essays|date=1969|publisher=Indiana University Press|pages=242–243|isbn=978-0-253-39503-0 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=vGEDAAAAMAAJ}}</ref> This style of writing, used in imperial edicts (宣命 ''senmyō'') preserved in the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' and other texts dating from the 8th century ([[:en:Nara period|Nara period]]), is known as ''senmyōgaki''.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Seeley|first1=Christopher|title=A History of Writing in Japan|date=1991|publisher=Brill|isbn=978-9004090811|pages=54–55|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KCZ2ya6cg88C&pg=PA54}}</ref>
==See also== {{wikisourcelang|ja|祓詞}} {{wikisourcelang|ja|大祓詞}} {{wikisourcelang|ja|六月晦大祓祝詞}}
*[[Kotodama]]
